summary: Mount St. Mary's president Simon Newman, a former private equity executive, fired two professors for disloyalty after they exposed his plan to manipulate college rankings. Newman wanted to 'cull' 20-25 freshmen before the enrollment deadline by using a student survey disguised as a personal motivation assessment. The goal was to boost the retention rate by 4-5% to improve US News & World Reports rankings. The scandal reveals how algorithmic ranking systems create perverse incentives that harm students, and raises questions about how widespread such gaming practices might be at other institutions.
key_points: A university president planned to use a deceptive student survey to identify freshmen to push out before enrollment deadlines, purely to game retention metrics
The survey was presented to students as measuring 'motivation, success, and happiness' with 'no wrong answers' while actually being used to identify students to expel
Professors who exposed the scheme were fired for 'disloyalty,' demonstrating how institutional power can silence whistleblowers
The US News & World Reports college ranking system creates incentives for schools to harm students rather than help them
The same tool (a survey algorithm) can be used for good or evil depending on institutional intent
money_quotes: Newman, a private equity asshole, got confused as to where he was working and decided to fire anyone who disagreed with him, referring to disloyalty as the cause.
This is hard for you because you think of the students as cuddly bunnies, but you can't. You just have to drown the bunnies.
A wonderful example of how an algorithm can be used for good or evil.
This gaming of the US News & World Reports model has got to stop, this shit is nuts.
It makes me wonder how many other places are doing stuff like this and not getting caught.
At least at this university the president was stupid enough to tell the professors the plan, right?
key_insight: College ranking algorithms create perverse incentives that lead institutions to harm students in order to game metrics, and the same survey tools can be weaponized against the very people they claim to help.
